---
title: "Analysis of Factors Affecting Well-Being"
author: "Vladyslav Rudenko"
date: last-modified
abstract: |
  This report analyses five independently collected datasets related to human
  well-being, stress, and productivity. Pearson correlation identifies variables
  with the strongest direct linear association with each target, while Principal
  Component Analysis (PCA) reveals the dominant structure of each dataset.
  Results consistently highlight sleep quality, stress level, physical activity,
  and work-life balance as key recurring factors across all five contexts.
  The analysis is purely exploratory: findings describe statistical associations,
  not causal relationships.
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from IPython.display import display, Markdown

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (9, 5),
    "figure.dpi": 110,
    "figure.facecolor": "white",
    "axes.facecolor": "#FAFAFA",
    "axes.edgecolor": "#D0D0D0",
    "axes.linewidth": 1.0,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "axes.axisbelow": True,
    "grid.color": "#E8E8E8",
    "grid.linestyle": "--",
    "grid.linewidth": 0.7,
    "axes.titlesize": 13,
    "axes.titleweight": "bold",
    "axes.labelsize": 11,
    "xtick.labelsize": 9,
    "ytick.labelsize": 9,
    "font.size": 10,
    "legend.frameon": False,
})

_ID_COLS = {"person_id", "employee_id"}

def numeric_features(df, target):
    num = df.select_dtypes(include="number").columns.tolist()
    return [c for c in num if c != target and c not in _ID_COLS]

def target_correlations(df, target):
    feats = numeric_features(df, target)
    return df[feats + [target]].corr(numeric_only=True)[target].drop(target)

def run_pca(df, target):
    feats = numeric_features(df, target)
    X = df[feats].dropna()
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
    pca = PCA()
    scores = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)
    loadings = pd.DataFrame(
        pca.components_.T,
        index=feats,
        columns=[f"PC{i}" for i in range(1, len(feats) + 1)],
    )
    return scores, loadings, pca.explained_variance_ratio_

def plot_correlations(df, target, title):
    corr = target_correlations(df, target).sort_values()
    colors = ["#B65E5A" if v < 0 else "#4C78A8" for v in corr.values]
    h = max(3.5, len(corr) * 0.45)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, h))
    ax.barh(corr.index, corr.values, color=colors, edgecolor="none")
    ax.axvline(0, color="#444", linewidth=1)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Pearson correlation")
    for i, v in enumerate(corr.values):
        ha = "left" if v >= 0 else "right"
        offset = 0.012 if v >= 0 else -0.012
        ax.text(v + offset, i, f"{v:.2f}", va="center", ha=ha, fontsize=9)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

def plot_variance(var_ratio, title):
    pcs = np.arange(1, len(var_ratio) + 1)
    cum = np.cumsum(var_ratio)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))
    bars = ax.bar(pcs, var_ratio, color="#4C78A8", width=0.7, edgecolor="none",
                  label="Per-component")
    ax.plot(pcs, cum, marker="o", color="#222", linewidth=2, label="Cumulative")
    ax.set_xticks(pcs)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Principal component")
    ax.set_ylabel("Variance ratio")
    for rect, v in zip(bars, var_ratio):
        ax.text(rect.get_x() + rect.get_width() / 2,
                rect.get_height() + 0.01, f"{v:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=8)
    ax.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

def plot_loadings(loadings, title):
    pc1 = loadings["PC1"].sort_values()
    colors = ["#B65E5A" if v < 0 else "#59A14F" for v in pc1.values]
    h = max(3.5, len(pc1) * 0.45)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, h))
    ax.barh(pc1.index, pc1.values, color=colors, edgecolor="none")
    ax.axvline(0, color="#444", linewidth=1)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("Loading")
    for i, v in enumerate(pc1.values):
        ha = "left" if v >= 0 else "right"
        offset = 0.012 if v >= 0 else -0.012
        ax.text(v + offset, i, f"{v:.2f}", va="center", ha=ha, fontsize=9)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

def plot_pc_scatter(scores, title, max_pts=3000):
    rng = np.random.RandomState(42)
    s = scores
    if len(s) > max_pts:
        idx = rng.choice(len(s), max_pts, replace=False)
        s = s[idx]
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5.5))
    ax.scatter(s[:, 0], s[:, 1], s=25, alpha=0.55,
               color="#4C78A8", edgecolors="none")
    ax.axhline(0, color="#BDBDBD", linewidth=1)
    ax.axvline(0, color="#BDBDBD", linewidth=1)
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.set_xlabel("PC1")
    ax.set_ylabel("PC2")
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

def corr_table(df, target):
    corr = target_correlations(df, target).sort_values(ascending=False)
    out = corr.reset_index()
    out.columns = ["Feature", "Pearson r"]
    out["Pearson r"] = out["Pearson r"].round(3)
    display(Markdown(out.to_markdown(index=False)))

DATA_DIR = "data/clean_data"
df_pca      = pd.read_csv(f"{DATA_DIR}/clean_pca.csv")
df_prod     = pd.read_csv(f"{DATA_DIR}/clean_productivity.csv")
df_sleep    = pd.read_csv(f"{DATA_DIR}/clean_sleep.csv")
df_students = pd.read_csv(f"{DATA_DIR}/clean_students.csv")
df_remote   = pd.read_csv(f"{DATA_DIR}/clean_remote.csv")
```

# Introduction

The goal of this project is to identify which lifestyle, sleep, and work-related
variables are most strongly associated with well-being, stress, and
productivity. The analysis is based on five separate datasets, each representing
a different population and context [@screentime2025; @sleep2023; @productivity2023;
@students2023; @remote2024]. Because the datasets come from different sources and
cannot be meaningfully merged, each one is analysed independently; the results
are then compared in @sec-comparison to surface recurring themes.

The two analytical methods applied throughout are Pearson correlation (described
in @sec-methods-corr) and Principal Component Analysis (described in
@sec-methods-pca). Both methods are implemented using the
scikit-learn library [@pedregosa2011] and data manipulation relies on
pandas [@mckinney2010].

# Datasets {#sec-datasets}

The five cleaned datasets used in this project are listed in @tbl-datasets.

| File | Description | Target variable |
|---|---|---|
| `clean_pca.csv` | General lifestyle and wellness data | `wellness` |
| `clean_productivity.csv` | Productivity and daily habits (n = 20 000) | `productivity` |
| `clean_sleep.csv` | Clinical sleep and health study | `stress_level` |
| `clean_students.csv` | Student lifestyle and academic outcomes | `stress_numeric` |
| `clean_remote.csv` | Remote-work conditions and mental health | `productivity_score` |

: Overview of the five analysed datasets. {#tbl-datasets}

Each dataset has its own target variable; this variable is excluded from the
predictor set during analysis and treated as the dependent measure. The
datasets vary considerably in size (100 to 20 000 observations) and in the
specific variables they contain, which is why a unified model is not appropriate.

# Methods {#sec-methods}

## Correlation Analysis {#sec-methods-corr}

For each dataset, the Pearson correlation coefficient is computed between every
numeric predictor and the selected target variable. The Pearson coefficient
measures the strength and direction of a **linear** relationship between two
continuous variables and is defined as:

$$
r_{xy} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}
              {\sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} \cdot
               \sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
$$ {#eq-pearson}

where $x_i$ and $y_i$ are paired observations, and $\bar{x}$, $\bar{y}$ are
their respective sample means. A value of $r = +1$ indicates a perfect positive
linear relationship; $r = -1$ a perfect negative one; $r = 0$ no linear
association. Results should not be interpreted as evidence of causation.

## Standardisation {#sec-methods-zscore}

Before PCA, all predictor columns are standardised to zero mean and unit
variance using the z-score transformation defined in @eq-zscore. This step is
essential because PCA is sensitive to the scale of the input variables — without
standardisation, variables with large numeric ranges would dominate the
principal components regardless of their statistical relevance.

$$
z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}
$$ {#eq-zscore}

where $\mu$ is the sample mean and $\sigma$ is the sample standard deviation
of the variable.

## Principal Component Analysis {#sec-methods-pca}

PCA is an unsupervised dimensionality-reduction method that transforms the
original $p$ numeric variables into a new orthogonal coordinate system
[@jolliffe2016]. The first principal component (PC1) captures the largest
possible fraction of the total variance; PC2 captures the most remaining
variance while being orthogonal to PC1, and so on.

The fraction of total variance explained by component $k$ is called the
**explained variance ratio** (EVR):

$$
\text{EVR}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}
$$ {#eq-evr}

where $\lambda_k$ is the $k$-th eigenvalue of the covariance matrix of the
standardised data (see @eq-zscore for standardisation).

Three PCA outputs are reported for each dataset: (1) the explained variance
plot (@eq-evr), (2) the PC1 loadings, and (3) a scatter of observations
in the PC1–PC2 plane.

# Results {#sec-results}

## Dataset 1: Lifestyle and Wellness {#sec-lifestyle}

**Target variable:** `wellness` | **Source:** @screentime2025

```{python}
#| label: tbl-pca-corr
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlations of lifestyle variables with `wellness`. Blue bars (positive r) indicate variables associated with higher wellness; red bars (negative r) indicate variables associated with lower wellness."
corr_table(df_pca, "wellness")
```

As shown in @tbl-pca-corr, sleep quality and productivity are the variables
most positively associated with the wellness score, while stress level shows
the strongest negative correlation, suggesting that higher stress is
consistently linked to lower well-being in this dataset.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlations between lifestyle variables and `wellness`. Bars to the right indicate positive associations; bars to the left indicate negative associations."
plot_correlations(df_pca, "wellness", "Lifestyle: Correlation with Wellness")
```

@fig-pca-corr visualises the same information graphically. The magnitude of the
bar reflects the strength of the association; the sign indicates its direction.
Variables with absolute $r$ values above 0.3 are considered practically
meaningful in exploratory analyses.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-var
#| fig-cap: "PCA explained variance for the lifestyle dataset. Bars show per-component variance; the line shows cumulative variance. The first two components together explain a substantial fraction of total variability."
scores_pca, loadings_pca, vr_pca = run_pca(df_pca, "wellness")
plot_variance(vr_pca, "Lifestyle: PCA Explained Variance")
```

@fig-pca-var shows how the total variance is distributed across principal
components. If the first two or three components account for a large share of
the total variance, the dataset has a compact low-dimensional structure that can
be summarised without losing much information.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-load
#| fig-cap: "PC1 loadings for the lifestyle dataset. Large absolute loading values indicate variables that contribute most strongly to the dominant pattern."
plot_loadings(loadings_pca, "Lifestyle: PC1 Loadings")
```

@fig-pca-load reveals which variables drive PC1 (the direction of maximum
variance). Variables with loadings of the same sign move together, while
variables with opposite signs move in opposing directions within the dominant
pattern.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pca-scatter
#| fig-cap: "PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the lifestyle dataset. Each point represents one observation projected into the two-dimensional PCA space."
plot_pc_scatter(scores_pca, "Lifestyle: PC1 vs PC2")
```

@fig-pca-scatter projects all observations into the PC1–PC2 plane. Visible
clusters or gradients would suggest meaningful subgroups in the data; a uniform
cloud would indicate that variation is continuous and unsegmented.

---

## Dataset 2: Productivity and Daily Habits {#sec-productivity}

**Target variable:** `productivity` | **Source:** @productivity2023

```{python}
#| label: tbl-prod-corr
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlations of daily-habit variables with `productivity` (n = 20 000)."
corr_table(df_prod, "productivity")
```

@tbl-prod-corr summarises the linear associations for the productivity dataset.
With 20 000 observations, even small correlation coefficients can be
statistically significant, so magnitude should be assessed alongside direction.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlations between daily-habit variables and `productivity`."
plot_correlations(df_prod, "productivity", "Productivity: Correlation with Productivity")
```

The pattern in @fig-prod-corr indicates which daily habits have the most
direct linear relationship with productivity. Variables that appear repeatedly
across datasets (e.g., sleep, stress) provide stronger evidence for their role
in well-being.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-var
#| fig-cap: "PCA explained variance for the productivity dataset."
scores_prod, loadings_prod, vr_prod = run_pca(df_prod, "productivity")
plot_variance(vr_prod, "Productivity: PCA Explained Variance")
```

@fig-prod-var shows the variance distribution across principal components for
the productivity dataset. The shape of the cumulative curve reveals how quickly
the most important dimensions can account for the majority of variability.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-load
#| fig-cap: "PC1 loadings for the productivity dataset."
plot_loadings(loadings_prod, "Productivity: PC1 Loadings")
```

@fig-prod-load identifies which predictors contribute most to PC1. If multiple
lifestyle-related variables (sleep, stress, exercise) load strongly on the same
component, this supports the hypothesis that well-being is multi-factorial rather
than driven by a single cause.

```{python}
#| label: fig-prod-scatter
#| fig-cap: "PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the productivity dataset (random subsample of 3 000 observations)."
plot_pc_scatter(scores_prod, "Productivity: PC1 vs PC2")
```

Due to the large sample size, @fig-prod-scatter displays a random subsample
of 3 000 observations to keep the plot readable without losing the overall
distributional shape.

---

## Dataset 3: Sleep and Stress {#sec-sleep}

**Target variable:** `stress_level` | **Source:** @sleep2023

```{python}
#| label: tbl-sleep-corr
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlations of sleep and health variables with `stress_level`. Non-numeric columns (gender, occupation, BMI category, blood pressure, sleep disorder) are excluded from the analysis."
corr_table(df_sleep, "stress_level")
```

As @tbl-sleep-corr shows, sleep quality and duration tend to correlate
negatively with stress level (higher sleep quality → lower stress), while
variables like heart rate may exhibit a positive association. Note that only
numeric columns are included; categorical variables such as occupation and
BMI category are excluded.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sleep-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlations between numeric sleep/health variables and `stress_level`."
plot_correlations(df_sleep, "stress_level", "Sleep: Correlation with Stress Level")
```

@fig-sleep-corr provides a visual overview of correlation directions. This
dataset is particularly relevant because the relationship between sleep and stress
is well-established in the clinical literature, making it a useful benchmark
for verifying that the analysis produces plausible results.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sleep-var
#| fig-cap: "PCA explained variance for the sleep dataset."
scores_sleep, loadings_sleep, vr_sleep = run_pca(df_sleep, "stress_level")
plot_variance(vr_sleep, "Sleep: PCA Explained Variance")
```

@fig-sleep-var shows the eigenvalue decay for the sleep dataset. A steep drop
after PC1 indicates that one dominant pattern accounts for most of the structure;
a gentler slope means the variance is spread more evenly across dimensions.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sleep-load
#| fig-cap: "PC1 loadings for the sleep dataset."
plot_loadings(loadings_sleep, "Sleep: PC1 Loadings")
```

@fig-sleep-load identifies the health and lifestyle variables that define the
primary direction of variation. Variables with large absolute loadings on PC1
are the main contributors to the dominant pattern in this dataset.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sleep-scatter
#| fig-cap: "PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the sleep dataset."
plot_pc_scatter(scores_sleep, "Sleep: PC1 vs PC2")
```

@fig-sleep-scatter shows how the 374 observations distribute in the
two-dimensional PCA space. The presence of visible bands or clusters would
suggest that the dataset contains distinguishable subgroups (e.g., people with
and without sleep disorders).

---

## Dataset 4: Student Lifestyle {#sec-students}

**Target variable:** `stress_numeric` | **Source:** @students2023

```{python}
#| label: tbl-students-corr
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlations of student lifestyle variables with `stress_numeric`."
corr_table(df_students, "stress_numeric")
```

@tbl-students-corr presents the associations for the student dataset.
Variables such as study hours per day, sleep hours, and GPA are typically
expected to relate to stress; the table shows whether these expectations are
confirmed in this sample.

```{python}
#| label: fig-students-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlations between student lifestyle variables and `stress_numeric`."
plot_correlations(df_students, "stress_numeric", "Students: Correlation with Stress")
```

@fig-students-corr shows which aspects of student life most strongly predict
the numeric stress score. This comparison is interesting because student stress
operates in a different context from clinical or workplace stress, yet some
common factors (sleep, activity) may still emerge.

```{python}
#| label: fig-students-var
#| fig-cap: "PCA explained variance for the student lifestyle dataset."
scores_students, loadings_students, vr_students = run_pca(df_students, "stress_numeric")
plot_variance(vr_students, "Students: PCA Explained Variance")
```

The variance decomposition in @fig-students-var indicates how many components
are needed to capture the main structure of the student dataset. Because the
dataset has only 6 predictor variables, the maximum number of components is 6.

```{python}
#| label: fig-students-load
#| fig-cap: "PC1 loadings for the student lifestyle dataset."
plot_loadings(loadings_students, "Students: PC1 Loadings")
```

@fig-students-load shows which student lifestyle variables dominate PC1.
If study hours and sleep hours load with opposite signs, it would suggest a
trade-off pattern: students who study more tend to sleep less, and vice versa.

```{python}
#| label: fig-students-scatter
#| fig-cap: "PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the student lifestyle dataset."
plot_pc_scatter(scores_students, "Students: PC1 vs PC2")
```

@fig-students-scatter maps the 2 000 student observations into the PC1–PC2
plane. A clear linear or curved structure would imply that student lifestyle
profiles vary along a well-defined continuum.

---

## Dataset 5: Remote Work {#sec-remote}

**Target variable:** `productivity_score` | **Source:** @remote2024

```{python}
#| label: tbl-remote-corr
#| tbl-cap: "Pearson correlations of remote-work variables with `productivity_score`. Non-numeric columns and boolean indicators are excluded; ID column `employee_id` is also dropped."
corr_table(df_remote, "productivity_score")
```

@tbl-remote-corr presents the correlations for the remote work dataset.
With only 100 observations, individual correlations should be interpreted
cautiously, but the overall pattern can still indicate which aspects of remote
work conditions most closely align with productivity.

```{python}
#| label: fig-remote-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pearson correlations between remote-work numeric variables and `productivity_score`."
plot_correlations(df_remote, "productivity_score", "Remote Work: Correlation with Productivity Score")
```

@fig-remote-corr shows whether variables such as work-life balance, sleep
hours, and burnout score exhibit direct linear associations with the remote
productivity score. A high absolute correlation for burnout would be consistent
with the expectation that burnout and productivity are inversely related.

```{python}
#| label: fig-remote-var
#| fig-cap: "PCA explained variance for the remote work dataset."
scores_remote, loadings_remote, vr_remote = run_pca(df_remote, "productivity_score")
plot_variance(vr_remote, "Remote Work: PCA Explained Variance")
```

@fig-remote-var shows how the 8 numeric predictor variables spread across
principal components. Because the dataset is small (100 rows), PCA results
should be treated as indicative rather than definitive.

```{python}
#| label: fig-remote-load
#| fig-cap: "PC1 loadings for the remote work dataset."
plot_loadings(loadings_remote, "Remote Work: PC1 Loadings")
```

@fig-remote-load identifies which remote-work predictors define the dominant
pattern. Variables loading strongly on PC1 are those that co-vary the most
across the 100 observed employees.

```{python}
#| label: fig-remote-scatter
#| fig-cap: "PC1 vs PC2 scatter for the remote work dataset."
plot_pc_scatter(scores_remote, "Remote Work: PC1 vs PC2")
```

@fig-remote-scatter shows the relatively small remote work sample in the
two-dimensional PCA space. Outliers or tight clusters would warrant closer
examination to understand whether they represent specific subgroups (e.g., by
work mode or job role).

---

# General Comparison {#sec-comparison}

@tbl-summary below summarises the strongest positive and negative correlation
with the target variable in each dataset. This cross-dataset view makes it
possible to identify variables that recur as important predictors across
multiple contexts.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Strongest positive and negative Pearson correlation with the target variable in each dataset."
_datasets_info = [
    ("Lifestyle",   df_pca,      "wellness"),
    ("Productivity",df_prod,     "productivity"),
    ("Sleep",       df_sleep,    "stress_level"),
    ("Students",    df_students, "stress_numeric"),
    ("Remote Work", df_remote,   "productivity_score"),
]

rows = []
for name, df, tgt in _datasets_info:
    corr = target_correlations(df, tgt)
    pos = corr[corr > 0]
    neg = corr[corr < 0]
    top_pos = f"{pos.idxmax()} ({pos.max():.2f})" if len(pos) > 0 else "—"
    top_neg = f"{neg.idxmin()} ({neg.min():.2f})" if len(neg) > 0 else "—"
    rows.append({
        "Dataset": name,
        "Target": tgt,
        "Strongest positive": top_pos,
        "Strongest negative": top_neg,
    })

display(Markdown(pd.DataFrame(rows).to_markdown(index=False)))
```

As @tbl-summary demonstrates, several variable types recur across datasets:

- **Sleep-related variables** appear in the lifestyle, sleep, student, and
  remote work datasets, consistently showing a positive relationship with
  well-being indicators.
- **Stress level** appears in multiple datasets as the strongest negative
  predictor, which is expected given that all targets are measures of
  well-being or performance.
- **Physical activity** shows positive associations in the lifestyle and
  student datasets.
- **Work-life balance** ranks highly in the remote work dataset.

This cross-dataset consistency provides stronger evidence that these factors
are genuinely associated with well-being, rather than being artefacts of any
single sample.

# Limitations

Several limitations should be acknowledged:

1. **Different populations and measurement scales.** The five datasets describe
   different groups of people using different instruments. Direct numeric
   comparison of correlation coefficients across datasets may be misleading.
2. **Correlation ≠ causation.** @eq-pearson measures linear association only.
   No causal inference is warranted from these results.
3. **Sample sizes vary greatly.** The remote work dataset has only 100
   observations, making its PCA results (see @sec-remote) less reliable than
   those for the productivity dataset (n = 20 000).
4. **Missing variables.** Stress, sleep, and activity are consistently
   important, but many potential confounders (age structure, socioeconomic
   status, cultural context) are absent from most datasets.

# Conclusion

This analysis examined five datasets related to human well-being, stress, and
productivity using Pearson correlation (@eq-pearson) and PCA
(@eq-evr). The standardisation step (@eq-zscore) ensured that PCA results
are scale-invariant.

Across all five datasets (summarised in @tbl-summary and @tbl-datasets), a
consistent picture emerges: **sleep quality and duration**, **stress level**,
**physical activity**, and **work-life balance** are the variables most
frequently and most strongly associated with the respective target variables.
These factors appear in contexts as different as student life, clinical sleep
studies, and remote work, which makes the pattern more robust.

The analysis has been conducted in a purely exploratory manner. All source code
is embedded in this document (use the "Code" toggle on any section to reveal it)
and can be reproduced by running `quarto render analysis.qmd` from the project
root, provided that the `data/clean_data/` directory is present.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
